prior.title: Hydrogen & Ammonia as Maritime Fuel
prior.summary: Green hydrogen production costs, ammonia bunkering infrastructure, vessel orders for ammonia/hydrogen-ready ships. Green hydrogen at $4-7/kg (target $2/kg by 2030). Owner: Sustainability + Operations.
prior.what_is_it: Green hydrogen production costs, ammonia bunkering infrastructure, vessel orders for ammonia/hydrogen-ready ships. Green hydrogen at $4-7/kg (target $2/kg by 2030). Ammonia bunkering planned at Singapore, Rotterdam, Yokohama. ~200 methanol-ready vessels on order.
prior.why_matters: Alternative maritime fuels determine which carriers can comply with FuelEU Maritime and IMO Net-Zero Framework at lowest cost. Operators who track fuel infrastructure development can identify which ports and carriers will offer green shipping options first.
key_data: Ammonia bunkering: planned at Singapore, Rotterdam, Yokohama, Kobe
FuelEU compliance: alternative fuels reduce GHG intensity penalty exposure
prior.full_brief:
# Hydrogen & Ammonia as Maritime Fuel

## Technology Profile

---

# 1. What's Being Tested or Deployed and By Whom

## Ammonia: First Commercial Engines and Demonstration Vessels

**Commercial engine arrival — WinGD**

FACT: "2025 also marks an important milestone with the commercial arrival of the first two-stroke engines." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

FACT: "WinGD reported <3ppm emissions for full-load testing earlier this year." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

*Operational implication:* For workspaces booking cargo on newbuild bulk carriers and tankers where charter parties are beginning to reference green fuel readiness, 2025 marks the point at which ammonia two-stroke engines cross from laboratory to commercial supply — the lead-time question now shifts from "is the engine available" to "which yards are ordering it and when do those vessels deliver."

**Demonstration vessels already operating**

FACT: "There are already demonstration vessels on the water using ammonia as a fuel, including small-scale supply vessels that began operations in 2023 and 2024." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Paving the Way for Ammonia as a Marine Fuel," January 27, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/paving-the-way-for-ammonia-as-a-marine-fuel-insights-into-the-imos-interim-guidelines/

**NOₓ test results across major engine developers**

FACT: "Testing results from a number of engine developers (including Wärtsilä, MAN ES, WinGD, and IHI) indicates that ammonia-fueled engine platforms will produce NOₓ emissions comparable to engines fueled by heavy maritime oil (in some cases, even lower)." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

*Analytical inference:* The parallel involvement of four major two-stroke and four-stroke engine developers signals that ammonia propulsion is not a single-vendor niche; competitive supply across MAN ES, WinGD, Wärtsilä, and IHI reduces single-source risk for shipowners and therefore for cargo interests selecting vessel partners.

**NYK Line and EXMAR: ammonia gas carriers under construction**

FACT: "Draft interim guidelines for using ammonia as cargo and fuel under the IGC Code are expected to be finalized at CCC 11 in September 2025, removing this prohibition and opening the door for ammonia-fueled, ammonia gas carriers to operate, such as those under construction by NYK Line and EXMAR." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Paving the Way for Ammonia as a Marine Fuel," January 27, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/paving-the-way-for-ammonia-as-a-marine-fuel-insights-into-the-imos-interim-guidelines/

**South Africa–Europe green shipping corridor consortium**

*Specific commissioning date and consortium membership details not available from the primary source block provided.* The Global Maritime Forum feasibility study on the South Africa–Europe iron ore corridor (listed as a corroborating source but not provided as a full source block) is flagged under New Sources Identified below; its claims cannot be carried as FACT spans here and are listed as a research gap for retrieval.

## Safety Standards and Alarm Thresholds

FACT: "Alarm thresholds are set at 25 ppm for localized alerts, 110 ppm for global alerts, and 220 ppm for system shutdowns." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Paving the Way for Ammonia as a Marine Fuel," January 27, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/paving-the-way-for-ammonia-as-a-marine-fuel-insights-into-the-imos-interim-guidelines/

FACT: "Storage of ammonia fuel in a refrigerated state at atmospheric pressure" is specified as a key provision of the interim guidelines. *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Paving the Way for Ammonia as a Marine Fuel," January 27, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/paving-the-way-for-ammonia-as-a-marine-fuel-insights-into-the-imos-interim-guidelines/

## Hydrogen: Status

*Specific hydrogen fuel operator deployment data and vessel-level trial results not available from the source blocks provided.* The IMO Future Fuels portal references hydrogen as a scalability and sustainability sub-topic alongside ammonia but does not provide operational deployment detail in the page content retrieved. *Source: IMO Future Fuels and Technology, https://futurefuels.imo.org/fuel-type/hydrogen This gap is flagged explicitly; hydrogen deployment status is a research gap requiring retrieval from the IMO portal's linked publications.

---

# 2. What This Tells Us About Industry Trajectory

**The regulatory inflection point is 2025**

FACT: "2025 is a critical year for the adoption of ammonia fuel in shipping." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

**IMO 2023 GHG strategy sets absolute targets that require alternative fuels**

FACT: "By setting absolute targets (20% below 2008 baseline levels in 2030, 70% in 2040 and net-zero in 2050), alternative fuels like ammonia are now necessary for ships to meet their regulatory requirements." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

FACT: "The IMO has officially set itself an alternative fuel uptake target: 'uptake of zero or near-zero GHG emission technologies, fuels and/or energy sources to represent at least 5%, striving for 10%, of the energy used by international shipping by 2030.'" *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

**FuelEU Maritime: the operative commercial pressure already in force**

FACT: "FuelEU Maritime targets all vessels above 5,000 gross tonnage calling at European ports, mandating GHG reductions in energy used on board. Beginning with a 2% reduction this year, by 2050 all such vessels will need to achieve an 80% reduction." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether FuelEU Maritime's banking, borrowing, and pooling mechanisms affect the compliance cost calculations for specific vessel charter parties the workspace operates under requires counsel review of the vessel's flag, route, and gross tonnage.

*Analytical inference:* The combination of IMO absolute GHG targets (2030/2040/2050) and FuelEU Maritime's annual intensity ratchet creates a two-track compliance pressure: global IMO measures will set the floor; EU port-call measures are already applying incremental cost pressure to vessels on Europe-touching trade lanes. For workspaces whose cargo moves on vessels calling at EU ports, the FuelEU compliance burden is live today, not future.

**N₂O: the unregulated emissions gap that will close**

FACT: "While N₂O is not technically regulated by the IMO (currently), its GHG potential means it will definitely be included in future emissions reporting, and thus controlled by future emissions regulations." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

*Analytical inference:* Engine developers' stated target of <3 ppm N₂O at full load (WinGD cited above) is the correct pre-competitive benchmark to track; once the IMO closes the N₂O gap in its well-to-wake framework, vessels unable to demonstrate N₂O control will face retroactive compliance exposure. Workspaces with Scope 3 reporting obligations under CSRD or client sustainability commitments should flag this gap in current supplier vessel questionnaires.

---

# 3. Supplier Access and Procurement Reality

## Regulatory Framework: What Has Been Issued and What Remains Pending

**MSC.1/Circ.1687: issued, non-mandatory**

FACT: "The IMO has issued MSC.1/Circ.1687, the Interim Guidelines for the Safety of Ships Using Ammonia as Fuel." The Maritime Safety Committee "approved the guidelines at its 109th session in December 2024." *Source: BIMCO, "IMO Issues Interim Guidelines for Safety of Ammonia-Fuelled Ships," March 27, 2025. https://www.bimco.org/news-insights/bimco-news/2025/03/27-ammonia/

FACT: "While the guidelines are non-mandatory, they align closely with the IGF Code and SOLAS principles, offering administrations and industry stakeholders a consistent reference point as ammonia-fuelled projects emerge." *Source: BIMCO, "IMO Issues Interim Guidelines for Safety of Ammonia-Fuelled Ships," March 27, 2025. https://www.bimco.org/news-insights/bimco-news/2025/03/27-ammonia/

**IGF Code: does not currently cover ammonia or hydrogen**

FACT: "The IGF Code does not cover low-flashpoint oil, methanol, ethanol, LPG, ammonia or hydrogen fuels." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Paving the Way for Ammonia as a Marine Fuel," January 27, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/paving-the-way-for-ammonia-as-a-marine-fuel-insights-into-the-imos-interim-guidelines/

**IGC Code: current prohibition on ammonia cargo as fuel**

FACT: "Under the current IGC Code, the use of toxic gas cargo for fuel (i.e. ammonia) is prohibited." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Paving the Way for Ammonia as a Marine Fuel," January 27, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/paving-the-way-for-ammonia-as-a-marine-fuel-insights-into-the-imos-interim-guidelines/

**Pending removal of IGC prohibition: CCC 11, September 2025**

FACT: "Draft interim guidelines for using ammonia as cargo and fuel under the IGC Code are expected to be finalized at CCC 11 in September 2025, formally removing the prohibition." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

SEVERITY: MONITORING — re-check window: October 2025, following CCC 11 outcome.

**Interim guidelines revision: CCC 12, 2026–2027**

FACT: "The interim guidelines are expected to undergo revisions during CCC 12, scheduled for 2026 or 2027." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Paving the Way for Ammonia as a Marine Fuel," January 27, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/paving-the-way-for-ammonia-as-a-marine-fuel-insights-into-the-imos-interim-guidelines/

**Seafarer training: fuel-specific ammonia guidelines initiated but unscheduled**

FACT: "HTW announced that a working group has been tasked with producing draft guidelines for the training of seafarers onboard ammonia-fueled ships. The guidelines will be presented at the next meeting of the HTW (currently unscheduled)." The agreed prioritization sequence for fuel-specific training is: "methyl/ethyl alcohol, ammonia, fuel cells, LPG, hydrogen, and batteries to be developed in that order." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

*Analytical inference:* Ammonia is second in the IMO's fuel-specific training queue behind methanol/ethanol, which means crew certification frameworks for ammonia-fuelled vessels will lag the engine commercialisation timeline. This creates a near-term bottleneck: vessels can be ordered and engines procured, but the pool of certified officers is constrained until HTW finalises and administrations implement the standards. Workspaces chartering or booking cargo on ammonia-fuelled vessels should query operators on crew certification status as part of vessel vetting.

**MSC 110: June 2025**

FACT: "In June, the Maritime Safety Committee meets for MSC 110." At MSC 110, "further discussions will be held on what changes need to be made to the IGF and IGC Codes to accommodate new fuels, and particularly the use of ammonia cargo as fuel." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

*Analytical inference:* MSC 110 (June 2025) is the first post-publication regulatory event that could narrow IGF/IGC Code gaps. Any workspace evaluating charter-party sustainability clauses referencing IMO code compliance should hold language review until post-MSC 110 outcomes are known.

## Cost Data

*Current production cost differentials between green ammonia/hydrogen and conventional bunker fuels are not available from the source blocks provided.* The AEA article references a UCL study on levy proposals and cost-benefit feasibility but does not reproduce cost figures in the retrieved content. This gap is flagged; cost comparison data requires retrieval from the IMO Future Fuels portal publication "Linking the employment of alternative marine fuels to a carbon price for shipping" referenced in the primary source.

---

# 4. Operational Fit by Transport Mode and Cargo Vertical

## Ocean (Primary Impact Mode)

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces whose supply chains depend on ocean freight — including bulk commodity, automotive, and high-value project cargo movements — ammonia and hydrogen as marine fuels are a vessel-operator-level procurement decision, not a cargo-owner decision today. The operational fit question for the workspace is whether the vessels chartered or booked have fuel-flexibility capability, CII rating visibility, and FuelEU Maritime compliance status.

**CII: operational pressure already present**

FACT: "CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator), a continuously-monitored assessment of a ship's energy efficiency, given in grams of CO₂ emitted per cargo-carrying capacity and nautical mile" is one of the three current regulatory measures for ships. *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces booking cargo on vessels with deteriorating CII ratings face the risk that operators slow-steam or reroute to manage their rating, affecting transit times. For time-sensitive cargo verticals (live events, humanitarian, film and TV equipment), CII-driven operational changes on the ocean leg are a schedule risk even before ammonia fuel is involved.

**Bunkering infrastructure: gap not yet resolved**

*Specific ammonia and hydrogen bunkering infrastructure availability by port and corridor is not available from the source blocks provided.* The BIMCO interim guidelines article references bunkering as a key provisions area but does not provide port-by-port infrastructure data. This is a research gap; the DNV source listed under New Sources Identified is the recommended retrieval candidate.

## Air (Secondary Mode — Limited Direct Applicability)

*Analytical inference:* Ammonia and hydrogen as maritime fuels have no direct applicability to the air freight modes that typically dominate workspaces prioritising air transport for high-value, time-sensitive cargo verticals. The indirect relevance is through Scope 3 reporting: if a workspace must account for ocean legs in its GHG inventory (CSRD, client requirements), the fuel type on the ocean segment affects the emissions figure reported.

## Road (Tertiary — No Direct Applicability)

*Analytical inference:* Road haulage connections to port are not affected by maritime fuel type. The road-to-port drayage segment operates under separate vehicle emissions standards. No connection to this technology brief.

---

# 5. Competitive Positioning Implications for the Workspace

**MEPC 83 outcome: GHG levy or fuel standard — the fork in the road**

FACT: "All eyes are on the upcoming meetings of the IMO's Maritime Environmental Protection Committee meeting in April this year (MEPC 83)." The two leading contenders are "a levy on all emissions" or "a goal-based fuel standard." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

FACT: "An extraordinary session of the committee will be held in October 2025, with the intention of formalising and adopting proposals discussed this April." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

SEVERITY: MONITORING — re-check window: November 2025, following MEPC extraordinary session outcome.

*Analytical inference:* The GHG levy path would create a cost pass-through mechanism that affects all ocean freight rates globally. The fuel standard path (FuelEU Maritime-style) would concentrate cost impact on EU-touching trade lanes. For workspaces with mixed trade lane exposure — Atlantic, trans-Pacific, intra-Asia — the choice of instrument materially changes where cost surcharges appear in the freight invoice and how early they arrive.

**FuelEU banking and pooling: competitive tool for carriers**

FACT: "A few alternatively-fueled vessels may thus be able to achieve the FuelEU Maritime goals of an entire fleet, encouraging shipowners to deploy these new technologies." *Source: Ammonia Energy Association, "Setting the Scene for Ammonia Maritime Fuel," March 21, 2025. https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/setting-the-scene-for-ammonia-maritime-fuel-regulatory-needs-and-timelines-to-decarbonize-shipping/

*Analytical inference:* Carriers that deploy early ammonia-fuelled tonnage can use FuelEU pool mechanics to cover a wider fleet, creating a compliance cost advantage over carriers without green-fuelled vessels. This is a competitive differentiation point: workspaces whose tender processes or sustainability commitments reward verified low-intensity ocean freight should query carriers on their FuelEU pooling strategy, not just their fuel type.

*Named competitor positioning:* No named competitor freight forwarder positions on ammonia maritime fuel are available from the source blocks provided. This gap is flagged; trade press monitoring (Lloyd's List, FreightWaves) is recommended.

---

# 6. Conversational and Strategic Talking Points

## What the Workspace Can Credibly Say

- "The IMO approved its first-ever interim safety guidelines for ammonia-fuelled vessels in December 2024 (MSC.1/Circ.1687). These are non-mandatory and goal-based, not yet prescriptive code."
- "Commercial two-stroke ammonia engines reached the market in 2025, with WinGD achieving sub-3 ppm N₂O at full-load testing."
- "The IGC Code currently prohibits ammonia cargo carriers from using their cargo as fuel. That prohibition is expected to be removed at IMO's CCC 11 meeting in September 2025."
- "FuelEU Maritime is already in force, applying a 2% GHG intensity reduction requirement in 2025, ratcheting to 80% by 2050, for all vessels above 5,000 GT calling EU ports."

## Questions to Pose to Ocean Carriers

- What is your fleet's current CII rating distribution, and how does your 2025 compliance position affect vessel availability on our lanes?
- Are any vessels on routes touching EU ports enrolled in a FuelEU Maritime pooling arrangement with ammonia or methanol-fuelled tonnage?
- What is your bunkering strategy for ammonia fuel on transoceanic routes once the IGC Code amendment is adopted?
- How are you tracking N₂O emissions from engine tests, and what is your plan once the IMO incorporates it into the well-to-wake framework?

## Pitfalls to Avoid

- *Industry interpretation:* Do not present MSC.1/Circ.1687 as mandatory regulation; it is a non-mandatory interim framework. Describing it as a compliance requirement the workspace or its carriers "must" meet overstates its current legal standing.
- Do not conflate "engine available" with "vessel available": the crew certification gap (HTW guidelines unscheduled) means commercially available engines do not translate directly to deployable, certified vessels at scale.
- Do not cite N₂O as currently regulated at IMO level; the source material is explicit that it is not yet regulated, only that it will be.

---

# 7. Time-to-Market, Procurement Window, and Action

## Confirmed Timeline (Sourced)

| Date | Event | Source |
|------|--------|--------|
| December 2024 | MSC 109 approves MSC.1/Circ.1687 interim ammonia safety guidelines | BIMCO, March 27 2025 |
| 2023–2024 | Small-scale ammonia supply vessel demonstrations commence | AEA, January 27 2025 |
| 2025 | Commercial two-stroke ammonia engines reach market | AEA, March 21 2025 |
| April 2025 | MEPC 83 — GHG mid-term measures debated | AEA, March 21 2025 |
| June 2025 | MSC 110 — IGF/IGC Code amendments discussed | AEA, March 21 2025 |
| September 2025 | CCC 11 — IGC Code prohibition on ammonia-as-fuel expected to be removed | AEA, January 27 2025 & March 21 2025 |
| October 2025 | IMO extraordinary MEPC session — GHG mid-term measures adoption | AEA, March 21 2025 |
| 2026 or 2027 | CCC 12 — interim guidelines revision | AEA, January 27 2025 |

*Analytical inference:* The September–October 2025 window (CCC 11 + extraordinary MEPC) is the single highest-density regulatory trigger point for ammonia maritime fuel commercialisation. If both events deliver as signalled — IGC prohibition removed and GHG pricing mechanism adopted — the commercial case for ordering ammonia-capable tonnage becomes materially clearer. Workspaces with multi-year ocean freight tender cycles that extend into 2026–2027 should structure sustainability clauses to allow for reference to this window's outcomes.

## Actions for the Workspace

1. **Before CCC 11 (September 2025):** Insert a review checkpoint into any ocean carrier tender or charter-party negotiation that covers voyages beyond 2026; the IGC Code outcome changes the vessel type universe available on ammonia-carrying routes.
2. **After extraordinary MEPC (October 2025):** Assess whether the adopted GHG mid-term measure is levy-based (universal cost impact) or fuel-standard-based (EU-lane concentrated); update ocean freight cost modelling accordingly.
3. **Carrier vetting now:** Add CII rating, FuelEU Maritime pooling status, and N₂O monitoring capability to standard vessel vetting questionnaires.
4. **Scope 3 data gap:** If the workspace has CSRD or client-driven Scope 3 reporting obligations, identify whether current carrier data feeds provide fuel-type information at voyage level; the shift to ammonia will change the WtW emissions factor applied.
5. **Monitor HTW scheduling:** When the next HTW session is announced (currently unscheduled), the ammonia crew training standard timeline will become clearer. Until then, treat crew certification as an unresolved bottleneck for large-scale ammonia fleet deployment.

---

*Note: The MDPI Sustainability source (Source 4) was listed as a corroborating source and is included in the source registry. The retrieved page content did not yield verbatim spans that could be span-grounded as FACT claims; its substantive detail (IMO WtW lifecycle mandate, H₂ storage challenges, ammonia NOx trade-offs) is therefore listed as a research gap in the New Sources Identified section below rather than asserted as FACT.*

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|-------------|-----|----------------------|------------------------|
| DNV – Ammonia as a Marine Fuel: Prospects and Challenges | https://www.dnv.com/expert-story/maritime-impact/ammonia-as-a-marine-fuel-prospects-and-challenges/ | 4 (Classification society — primary technical authority on ship safety and fuel approval pathways) | DNV is a lead classification society whose approval-pathway analysis for ammonia/hydrogen ships is required to ground FACT claims on IGF/IGC Code compliance gaps and regulatory barrier timelines not fully available in the AEA and BIMCO blocks |
| Ammonia Energy Association – IMO Interim Guidelines Deep-Dive | https://ammoniaenergy.org/articles/paving-the-way-for-ammonia-as-a-marine-fuel-insights-into-the-imos-interim-guidelines/ | 4 (Industry body analysis — already used as Source 5 above) | Already in source pool; listed here for completeness as the deep-dive companion to the regulatory timeline article |
| Global Maritime Forum – South Africa–Europe Iron Ore Green Shipping Corridor Feasibility Study | https://globalmaritimeforum.org/press/south-africa-europe-shipping-route-could-run-on-green-ammonia-by-2026/ | 3 (Intergovernmental-adjacent industry body — primary source for named corridor consortium) | Required to ground FACT claims on Anglo American / CMB.TECH / ENGIE consortium, 2029 ammonia bulk carrier target, and full decarbonisation by 2030s; not available as a source block in this run |
| MDPI Sustainability – Hydrogen & Ammonia as Future Clean Marine Energies | https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/24/11364 | 6 (Peer-reviewed academic — December 2025) | Required to ground FACT claims on IMO WtW lifecycle mandate scope, H₂ storage density challenges, ammonia toxicity/NOx trade-off quantification; page content retrieved was a journal index, not full article text; full paper retrieval needed |

---
